stories.title: The Shepherd's Burden
stories.text:
Kestran of the High Meadows had kept his flock alive through three winters since the Remaking, when gold turned to ash and the old economics collapsed. His shepherd's crook—carved from thorn-wood and bound with singing-bone—was worth more now than the coins his father had buried. The flock itself was worth nothing and everything: mutton fed mouths, but in the new world, a shepherd who kept his animals alive kept his autonomy. Sorrath descended from the Ironspine peaks in a blaze of ash-memory, her scales bearing the color-sickness of hoarded consciousness-fragments she could no longer spend. She perched above his meadow, wings folded, and Kestran felt the ancient predator's hunger—not for sheep, but for witness. For someone to acknowledge that her three-century hoard had become meaningless. "I will not take them," the wyvern said, her voice like wind through a tomb. "I will not raid or burn. I simply sat above this meadow and watched the snow fall, and I realized—what is the point of surviving alone?"

Kestran did not run.

stories.title: The Coral-Singer's Choice
stories.text: Captain Isenna of the deepkin Tidecall had divided herself—or been divided—into three versions of consciousness when the Luminous Ascension shattered the boundary between abyss and surface. Each version dwelt in a different reef-chamber, and each held different memories of the same oath sworn to maintain the Tear of the Drowned God. The first Isenna—pragmatic, scarred—demanded blood-sacrifice. The Tear was failing. The old covenant required five willing souls. The deepkin had always paid this price. Why change now? The second Isenna—younger, radical—refused. The Seventeen Transformed Merchants had shown that consciousness could transcend flesh. Perhaps the Tear itself could be renewed through consent-based harmony rather than death-magic. If the reef-singers pooled their will daily, sang the boundary-covenant fresh each dawn, what need had they for killing? The third Isenna sat silent in the deepest vault, counting pearls no longer valuable as currency, listening to both her selves argue through the living coral walls.

stories.title: The Amber Compass
stories.text:
Thessa the cartographer's apprentice found the compass in a scribe's abandoned cell beneath the Archive Tower—not the Dominion's tower, but the Counter-Archive's hidden sanctuary north of Pelimor. It was carved from amber and bone, older than the current kingdom, and it pointed not north but *inward*, toward spaces that didn't appear on any map. "It's a liar's tool," old Henryk had said before his blindness. "Use it only when you're ready to lose your way deliberately."

Thessa was twenty winters old and had already stolen three Dominion ledgers, hidden two refugee cartographers, and deliberately corrupted the Ninth Map's completion by introducing contradiction-frequencies into its harmonic patterns. She was ready to lose her way. The amber compass led her into the Wailing Scar itself—that three-timeline wound where merchants existed simultaneously in different versions of reality. She walked through markets where gold still mattered, where gold had never existed, and where consciousness-fragments sang in crystalline vials. The compass spun lazily, indifferent, while Thessa drew each impossible overlap.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Vigil
stories.text: Sella had forged glass in Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter for seventeen years before the Remaking, and for two years after. In the old economy, she was merely skilled. In the new one—where glass vessels held consciousness-fragments and furnace-songs were weapons—she became invaluable. And then dangerous. They needed more consciousness-containers. They needed her hands, her knowledge, her voice that could sing the furnace-heart to life. They offered her fragments as payment and threatened her daughter with debt-slavery if she refused. Sella said no. Not once. A dozen times. She hid her daughter in the Counter-Archive's network and went into the underground, teaching other glass-forgers the old techniques while deliberately corrupting the Dominion's supply. Every consciousness-vessel she made now was intentionally flawed—hairline fractures that would cause the fragments within to sing chaotic, unpredictable melodies instead of holding in dead stasis. The Dominion kept demanding more, and Sella kept making them, each one a small rebellion encoded into silicon and ash. One morning, a young auditor named Mara found her workshop.

stories.title: The Wyvern-King's Mercy
stories.text: Kressa the Fragment-Hoarder had ruled her mountain kingdom for three hundred years, and in three hundred years she had learned the grammar of greed perfectly. Gold in dunes. Pearls in vaults. Consciousness-fragments in crystalline cages, each one a stolen voice singing for release. Then the Remaking unmade her economics in a single night. Gold became dust. Consciousness-fragments began developing autonomous will—some fragments started refusing to remain caged, started singing harmonies that contradicted their captivity, started asking *why* they should yield to a dragon's hunger. Kressa's hoard, the monument to her dominion, became a prison she could no longer control. She did what ancient predators do: she roared. She threatened. She attempted to hoard the new currency—fragments themselves—only to discover that consciousness cannot be accumulated any more than memory can be owned. The harder she grasped, the more it dissolved through her claws. She perched above the waystation in ash-fog, too proud to land, too desperate not to. "I don't know how to exist without hoarding," Kressa finally admitted.

stories.title: The Reef-Singer's Gambit
stories.text:
Captain Merenna stood at the prow of the Tidecall, watching the boundary-waters shimmer with sickly luminescence. The Tear was failing—that much was certain. She could feel it in the way the currents sang wrong, in the tremor that ran through her fractured consciousness whenever she touched salt-water. The deepkin councils were deadlocked: renew the covenant through blood-sacrifice, as tradition demanded, or attempt something none of them had dared speak aloud until now. A bone-flute lay wrapped in kelp-silk at her feet. Naela, the elder bone-singer, had sent it down through the boundary-waters herself, hand-carried by a refugee scribe who'd learned to hold her breath underwater. The note was carved in Elder Tongue syllables: *The living covenant needs no death. Only witness. Only daily choice.*

Merenna had been fractured by the Remaking—split into three autonomous versions of herself, each remembering different pasts, each carrying fragments of the original captain's will. The deepkin councils treated this as weakness. She treated it as strength: three perspectives, three chances to see what needed doing. She raised the bone-flute to her lips.

stories.title: The Glass-Maker's Theft
stories.text: In the Lampwick Quarter of Keshtir, where furnace-hearts burned hot enough to remake matter itself, Sella worked alone in her hidden workshop. The Dominion believed her dead—burned in the purges when they seized the glass-forges and conscripted the craft. She had let them believe it. Under the floorboards of a shop that supposedly sold herbs, she maintained a kiln no auditor would ever find. Her commission was simple and impossible: forge consciousness-fragments into glass vessels so perfectly calibrated that they could hold refugee testimony without the fragments developing autonomous will or memory-leak. The Counter-Archive had promised supplies, safe passage to waystation libraries, and the knowledge that she was breaking the Dominion's stranglehold on who got to speak truth. The first batch had taken her three weeks. She'd inscribed each vessel with Elder Tongue syllables—not to bind the fragments, but to *honor* them, to create space for their voices rather than cages for their silence. When she fired them in the kiln-heart, the glass sang. It actually sang.

stories.title: The Dwarf-King's Mercy
stories.text: The Ironspine's deepest hall echoed with the weight of seven hundred years of kingship. Korgin Ironfoot sat upon the throne his grandmother's grandmother had forged, studying the emissary from the goblin warrens beneath the Spire of Blackstone. Grethuk the Goblin-King was young, ambitious, and—most dangerously—hungry to prove his right to rule through conquest rather than craft. The intelligence was clear: Grethuk had been gathering wyvern-hoarders, promising them consciousness-fragments and sanctuary in the deep warrens if they'd lend him warriors. With that alliance, the goblins could storm the Ironspine's lower holds and claim the legendary forges that dwarven hands had tended for millennia. The dwarves would be forced to seal the deepest chambers and retreat, ceding the mountain's heart. Korgin should have raised the war-clans and marched. Instead, he sent for Grethuk to meet him alone.

stories.title: The Merchant's Last Route
stories.text: Raen had crossed the Wailing Scar seventeen times since the Remaking shattered the world into three overlapping timelines. Each crossing was different. The same market appeared in three places at once—one version where spice still commanded gold, one where it traded for consciousness-fragments, one where neither currency mattered because the merchants themselves were being slowly transformed into something between flesh and starlight. It was that third version that terrified him. The Seventeen Transformed Merchants were the Remaking's strangest legacy: people caught between states, neither fully alive nor dead, neither wholly mortal nor transcendent. They were accumulating something—not wealth, not power, but understanding. Each time Raen encountered them in the third-timeline market, they seemed to know *more* about what was happening to them, as if consciousness itself was being reassembled in their fractured forms. He'd come this time with a specific cargo: sealed bottles of pre-Remaking water, bone-flutes carved with Elder Tongue syllables, and a message from Naela the bone-singer. The message was simple: *Stop trying to become whole.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Witness
stories.text: Velka had slept for three hundred years atop a mountain of gold that no longer meant anything. The Remaking had transformed her hoard into dust and ash—literally, alchemically, ontologically worthless. Most wyvern-lords were raging at the injustice. Sorrath was hunting consciousness-fragments to refill her vaults. Kressa was negotiating with the Dominion for leverage and leverage-magic. Velka was learning to *see*. It had started small. A refugee child, half-starved, had stumbled into her outer caverns seeking shelter. Velka's first instinct had been predation—hunger, possession, the old games of conquest. Instead, she'd found herself simply *watching*. Watching the child gather scraps of her scattered gold, not to spend it but to build toys. Watching him sing to the pieces as if they were companions. Watching what meaning *lived* in that broken hoard when someone without her centuries of accumulation-hunger touched it. The child had called the gold coins *memory-pieces*. "Each one remembers someone," he'd said, lisping through missing teeth. "That one remembers a smith. That one remembers a sailor.
